title: Locally Maximal Closed Orbits of Reeb Flows
speaker: Marco Mazzucchelli
abstract: A compact invariant set of a flow is called locally maximal when it is the largest invariant set in some neighborhood. In this talk, based on joint work with Erman Cineli, Viktor Ginzburg, and Basak Gurel, I will present a "forced existence" result for the closed orbits of certain Reeb flows on spheres of arbitrary odd dimension: - If the contact form is non-degenerate and dynamically convex, the presence of a locally maximal closed orbit implies the existence of infinitely many closed orbits.

proof_stages.label: Forced-existence paradigm
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames the result as a forced-existence theorem: the presence of a special orbit in a symplectic or contact dynamical system should force infinitely many closed or periodic orbits.

proof_stages.label: Locally maximal Reeb-orbit setting
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines locally maximal, equivalently isolated, invariant sets and specializes the discussion to Reeb flows on standard contact spheres realized by star-shaped hypersurfaces.

proof_stages.label: Hypotheses and two main theorems
proof_stages.summary: After stating nondegeneracy and dynamical-convexity/index assumptions, the speaker formulates two related results: a locally maximal closed Reeb orbit, or a hyperbolic one under weaker index hypotheses, forces infinitely many closed Reeb orbits.

proof_stages.label: Precedents and scope of novelty
proof_stages.summary: Known low-dimensional and Hamiltonian-diffeomorphism analogues are reviewed to explain that the theorem is new for higher-dimensional Reeb flows and is inspired by earlier Floer-theoretic arguments.

proof_stages.label: Symplectic homology and barcode vanishing
proof_stages.summary: Filtered symplectic homology of the contact sphere is introduced as the main tool, and displaceability of the filling gives vanishing in the full action range, equivalently a uniform action-shift constant that kills the filtered groups.

proof_stages.label: Iteration-index control
proof_stages.summary: Properties of the Maslov/Conley–Zehnder indices under iteration, together with the finiteness assumption, are used to choose a large iterate whose possible Floer boundary partners have action difference either very small or very large.

proof_stages.label: Uniform crossing-energy bound
proof_stages.summary: Local maximality supplies a uniform lower bound, independent of iteration, on the energy of any Floer cylinder crossing a fixed neighborhood of the iterated orbit.

proof_stages.label: Floer-homological contradiction
proof_stages.summary: Assuming only finitely many closed orbits, the chosen iterate defines a nonzero cycle in an action window and remains nonzero after the barcode shift, contradicting the vanishing map from displaceability.

examples.name: Transverse homoclinic point of a hyperbolic fixed point
examples.note: A transverse intersection of stable and unstable manifolds is recalled as a classical forced-existence mechanism producing symbolic dynamics and many periodic points, illustrating the general paradigm rather than generating the Reeb proof.
examples.engagement_reason: he draws the hyperbolic fixed point with stable and unstable manifolds meeting transversely and explains how the resulting horseshoe/symbolic dynamics gives many periodic points

examples.name: Anosov–Katok pseudo-rotation on S^2
